fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get the lambda bridge adapter wrapped up. Basically the idea is that our Rails app should be able to run behind AWS gateways without us having to rewrite a bunch of stuff every time we deploy to a new gateway type. Right now every project has its own messy glue code and things keep breaking — cookies get lost, binary files come back garbled, error pages don't show up right, that kind of thing. We want one clean adapter layer that just handles all the translation under the hood.

The tricky part is we support a few different gateway shapes — you know, the newer HTTP one, the older HTTP one, the REST proxy style, and the load balancer. They all have slightly different response conventions, especially around how cookies get returned (remember how we handled the multi-value thing in that older API version? same idea here). Binary responses need special encoding treatment.

We also need it to handle the non-HTTP stuff — job queues, event bus messages, that maintenance runner thing we scoped out, and the parameter config export. And there's an installer flow for generating deployment scaffolding for both gateway modes.

There's a debug toggle that should only activate under certain env conditions — it's gated similarly to how we did it in the dev-only config check last quarter. Make sure the environment mapping strips stage prefixes properly too. Overall the output contract for each gateway type needs to stay stable and predictable.

trigger_keywords: how are cookies returned for http v2 vs http v1
what is the difference in cookie response format between gateway versions
why do cookies look different depending on the gateway type
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For HTTP API payload v2, multiple Set-Cookie values are joined with a pipe delimiter and emitted as a single 'cookies' field. For HTTP API payload v1, multiple Set-Cookie values are returned as separate repeated headers represented as 'set_cookie_multi' joined by pipe. The REST and ALB gateways also use repeated/multi-value header structures for cookies. Each gateway version has a distinct response shape for cookies and must not be conflated.

trigger_keywords: how does binary image encoding work
when should base64 be used in the response
how is a PNG or binary file body returned to the gateway
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Binary responses (e.g., image/png) must be base64-encoded and the response must set isBase64Encoded (or equivalent) to true. The decoded body must match a stable SHA-256 digest: 93ae7d494fad0fb30cbf3ae746a39c4bc7a0f8bbf87fbb587a3f3c01f3c5ce20. Non-binary text responses must explicitly set base64_encoded=false for gateways that include that field (ALB always includes it; HTTP API v2 only includes it when true).

trigger_keywords: how does the debug gate work
when is debug mode turned on
what conditions enable debug output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Debug mode is enabled only when two conditions are simultaneously true: the rack environment is 'development' (rack_env=development), AND the incoming request query string includes 'debug=1'. If the rack_env is not development, or if the debug=1 query parameter is absent, debug_enabled must be false. The app_env field is not the controlling variable — rack_env is.

trigger_keywords: how are stage prefixes handled in the path
should the API gateway stage name appear in the path
what does path stripping do for routing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Stage prefixes included in incoming API Gateway paths (e.g., /prod/ or /staging/) must be stripped before the path is passed to the application as PATH_INFO. The resulting PATH_INFO should be the clean application path (e.g., '/') and SCRIPT_NAME should be empty. This ensures application routing logic does not need to be aware of deployment stage names.

trigger_keywords: how does the load balancer environment handle multiple forwarded-for values
what happens when x-forwarded-for has multiple IPs in ALB events
how are multi-value headers handled in the ALB environment mapping
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For ALB events with multi-value headers, the X-Forwarded-For header values must be joined into a single comma-separated string (e.g., '72.218.219.201, 72.218.219.201, 34.195.252.132'). For all other multi-value headers, only the first scalar value is used. This asymmetry is intentional and specific to the forwarded-for field.

trigger_keywords: what commands are allowed in the runner
how does the runner reject disallowed commands
what happens when a runner command is not on the allowlist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The runner only executes commands that match a configured allowlist pattern (e.g., './bin/rake ...' style commands). Commands that do not match the allowlist must not be executed and must return an error response with error=unknown_runner_command and the raw command string. Allowed commands that fail at the OS level return status, empty headers, and stdout/body signals from the actual process output.

trigger_keywords: how does parameter store export work
what is the difference between env_overwrite and env_preserve modes
how does parameter export handle existing environment variables
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Parameter store export supports three modes: 'env_overwrite' sets all parameter values into the process environment unconditionally; 'env_preserve' sets parameter values only for keys not already present in the environment (existing values like FOO=test are preserved); 'dotenv' renders parameter name/value pairs as dotenv-format lines regardless of the current environment. All three modes output KEY=value lines.

trigger_keywords: what does the installer generate
what files does the deployment installer create
what is the difference between http_gateway and rest_gateway installer modes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Both 'http_gateway' and 'rest_gateway' installer modes produce the same set of output signals: an installer banner, a handler file containing the bridge reference, a SAM/CloudFormation template with a Transform and Description, a build script that installs bundle dependencies, and deploy scripts that package an image and use environment variables for stack deployment. The output contract is identical for both modes; the difference lies in the generated template/handler internals.

trigger_keywords: how are event bridge messages handled
what happens when a non-HTTP event bus event arrives
how does the adapter process EventBridge source/detail payloads
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: EventBridge (event bus) events are non-HTTP events identified by having a source, detail-type, and detail payload. They are dispatched to a configured event callback (not the web application request pipeline). The handler must log an entry containing the event identifier, and the test verifies log_contains_event_id=true. No HTTP response is produced.

trigger_keywords: how does the SQS queue job handler work
what does the queue job adapter do with active job payloads
what is returned after processing a queue message
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Queue (SQS) job events contain an Active Job payload. The adapter must execute the job, which prints a job work signal to stdout (stdout_has_job=true). After processing, the response must include a batch item failures list that is empty (batch_failures=0). Failed items would appear in the list; success means the list is empty.

trigger_keywords: how does the REST gateway handle a 500 exception
what is returned when the application raises an error for REST proxy
does the error page include the public 500 template for REST
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the application raises an unhandled exception under the REST API proxy gateway, the response must have status=500, content_type=text/html; charset=UTF-8, body_has_error_page=true, and body_has_public_500=true. The public 500 error page (a static HTML file served from the public directory) must be included in the body. This behavior must be consistent with the ALB exception scenario.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'gateway_request', 'gateway_env', 'debug_gate', 'parameter_store', 'runner', 'event_bridge', 'queue_job', 'installer'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: remember how we handled the multi-value thing in that older API version
context_pointers.actual_meaning: HTTP API payload v1 uses multiValueHeaders in the response shape, returning Set-Cookie as a repeated array under the 'set_cookie_multi' key joined by pipe, as tested in feature1_2_http_api_v1_requests.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the debug toggle gated similarly to how we did it in the dev-only config check last quarter
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Debug is gated on rack_env=development AND query param debug=1 simultaneously, as specified in feature3_debug_gate.json — rack_env is the environment discriminator, not app_env

context_pointers.vague_reference: that maintenance runner thing we scoped out
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The runner command dispatcher in feature5_runner_commands.json — executes allowlisted shell commands (e.g., ./bin/rake) and rejects others with error=unknown_runner_command
AWS SSM Parameter Store integration in feature4_parameter_store_exports.json supporting three modes: env_overwrite, env_preserve, and dotenv rendering

The PATH_INFO in the rack environment must have the API Gateway stage segment removed, resulting in SCRIPT_NAME='' and PATH_INFO='/' as shown in feature2_1_http_api_environment.json and feature2_2_rest_api_environment.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can speak to directly — you'll want to look at the existing test fixtures and the gateway-specific behavior already scoped out in the project. The key thing from my side is that the output contract stays stable and predictable for each gateway type; the specifics of how that's implemented are for the engineering team to work out from the existing specs.

feature1_1_http_api_v2_requests.json.description: HTTP API payload version 2 events are translated into web requests and returned as gateway-compatible response fields for normal pages, HEAD requests, multiple cookies, binary image bodies, public static files, redirects, application errors, and login session follow-up.

cache_control=max-age=0, private, must-revalidate
body_has_hello=true
logged_in=false
body_has_error_page=false
body_has_public_500=false

body_has_hello=true
logged_in=false
body_has_error_page=false
body_has_public_500=false

feature3_debug_gate.json.description: Debug mode is enabled only when a development environment is active and the incoming query includes debug=1; missing the query flag disables debug output even in development.

cases.expected_output: debug_enabled=true
debug_enabled=false

feature4_parameter_store_exports.json.description: Named parameter values can be exported to process environment variables with configurable overwrite behavior or rendered as dotenv file lines.

cases.expected_output:
FOO=foo
BAR=bar

FOO=test
BAR=bar

FOO=foo
BAR=bar

feature5_runner_commands.json.description: Runner events execute allowed maintenance command patterns and expose their exit status, empty headers, stdout signal, and response body signal; disallowed commands are rejected with a normalized error category and the raw command.

cases.expected_output:
status=1
headers_empty=true
stdout_has_unknown_task=true
body_has_unknown_task=true

error=unknown_runner_command
command=ls -lAGp

feature6_1_event_bus_events.json.description: Non-HTTP event bus messages with source, detail type, and detail payload are handled by the configured event callback and emit a log containing the event identifier.

cases.expected_output: log_contains_event_id=true

feature6_2_queue_job_events.json.description: Queue job events containing an active job payload are executed, print the job work signal, and return an empty batch failure list.

cases.expected_output:
stdout_has_job=true
batch_failures=0

feature7_deployment_installers.json.description: Installation tasks for both HTTP and REST gateway deployment modes create handler, infrastructure template, build script, and deployment scripts with the expected deployment signals.

handler_contains_bridge=true
template_has_transform=true
template_has_description=true
build_script_exists=true
build_script_installs_bundle=true
deploy_scripts_exist=true
deploy_packages_image=true
deploy_stack_uses_env=true
